title: Party membership and community participation in urban China
abstract: Grassroots branches of the Communist Party of China in urban neighborhoods play a vital role in motivating residents to participate in community affairs. In this article, we examine the role of communist Party membership in community participation in Shanghai, based on data from the Shanghai Urban Neighborhood Survey (SUNS). We categorize Party members into three subgroups according to their affiliations: community Party members (affiliated with residential communities), unit Party members (affiliated with work units), and non-local Party members (affiliated with other entities), and compare their community participation to those of nonparty members. The results show that community Party members and unit Party members are more likely to participate in community affairs than nonparty members, whereas non-local Party members are not significantly different from nonparty members. Community participation among Party members is influenced by both individual-level and community-level factors. Notably, in communities where the administrative burden on community organizations is high, participation by community Party members may be inhibited. In contrast, greater organizational autonomy enhances the participation of unit Party members.
authors_text: Yu, Yue; He, Rui; Cai, Jiewei; Li, Jun
